regions: Nearctic — Gulf Coastal Plain and lower Mississippi River basin
nativeRange.habitat: Generally found in shallow, slow-moving or standing water — swamps, marshes, oxbow ponds, ditches, lakes, and sluggish streams — typically over muddy substrate with abundant aquatic vegetation. Has been reported as a non-obligate burrower that constructs simple burrows during dry periods rather than living in them year-round.

decorPreferences: tightly-fitting lid (accomplished climber — escape risk is high)
abundant hiding places — at least two cover spots per individual
PVC pipes, cholla wood, and small caves
leaf litter (oak, almond, beech) for biofilm grazing and shelter
fine sand or smooth gravel substrate
live or artificial plants — generally well-tolerated, unlike larger crayfish
sponge filter or intake-guarded filter to protect juveniles

diet.notes: Generally considered an opportunistic omnivore and detritivore. Has been reported to accept sinking pellets, algae wafers, blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach, peas), leaf litter, and protein items such as frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, and small pieces of fish or shrimp. Adults tend to do well on three to four feedings per week while juveniles are commonly fed daily. Calcium-rich supplements (cuttlebone, crushed eggshell, mineral stones) are widely recommended to support successful molting.

goodWith: small peaceful schooling fish that occupy the mid- and upper water column (neon tetras, ember tetras, endlers)
dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina) with caution — adequate cover required and some predation is to be expected
conspecifics — generally considered one of the most social and peaceful crayfish species, often kept in small colonies
avoidWith: large or aggressive fish (cichlids, large gouramis)
bottom-dwelling fish that compete for territory (larger loaches, larger plecos)
long-finned fish (fancy guppies, bettas, angelfish) which may have fins nipped
other crayfish species, especially larger ones (will be outcompeted or eaten)
African dwarf frogs and similar slow bottom-dwellers

summary: A tiny, peaceful North American crayfish reaching only 3–4 cm. One of the most social and beginner-friendly crayfish, suitable for small species-only tanks or planted community setups with appropriate tankmates.
careNotes: The Cajun Dwarf Crayfish is one of the smallest and most peaceful crayfish in the hobby and is widely considered a good first crayfish for hobbyists comfortable with basic invertebrate care. Wild-type animals are typically described as brown-grey to reddish-brown with darker longitudinal striping or fine spotting along the carapace and abdomen; selectively bred colour forms (notably an orange-pink form and a blue form) are also sold under the same scientific name. Adults rarely exceed about 3–4 cm body length, so a trio can be comfortably kept in a 20 L (~5 US gal) aquarium, with 40 L (~10 US gal) often suggested for a small colony of 5–10 individuals. A tightly-fitting lid is important — like nearly all crayfish, this species is a capable climber and will escape through any gap in the cover, filter intake slot, or HOB return slot. Husbandry-wise it is undemanding: temperature anywhere in the ~18–26 °C range is generally accepted (the species naturally experiences a very wide thermal range in the wild, from near-freezing winter water in the northern part of its range to 30 °C in summer swamps), pH 6.0–8.0, and most aquarium hardness values. As with all crustaceans it is highly sensitive to copper-based fish medications and snail treatments, which should be avoided entirely. Cross-source notes: the designated primary source (The Shrimp Farm) returned HTTP 403 Forbidden and the designated secondary (Practical Fishkeeping) also returned HTTP 403; primary husbandry data was sourced from Aquarium Breeder and cross-checked against Aquatic Arts (retailer care sheet), Aquariadise (genus-level Cambarellus caresheet), and the USGS NAS species profile for native-range and habitat information.
breedingNotes: Generally considered easy to breed — reproduction tends to happen on its own once a sexually mature pair is housed together with adequate cover. Females become gravid at roughly 18 mm total length (around two months of age in favourable conditions) and carry 15–75 eggs (averaging around 45) attached to the swimmerettes under the abdomen for approximately 3–4 weeks until hatching. Newly-hatched juveniles remain on the mother for the first few days before dispersing and feeding on biofilm, leaf-litter detritus, and crushed pellet food. Breeding has been reported year-round in captivity with peaks loosely tracking the wild December–March and May–July reproductive seasons. Calcium-rich supplementation is helpful for successful molting in breeding females.

primary.name: The Shrimp Farm
primary.notes: Designated primary source returned HTTP 403 Forbidden via WebFetch on the homepage; no species-specific URL was directly accessible. Primary husbandry data was sourced from Aquarium Breeder (Shrimp and Snail Breeder) instead; URL retained for reviewer reference.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping
additional.notes: Designated secondary source returned HTTP 403 Forbidden on the homepage. Substituted with Aquarium Breeder, Aquatic Arts, Aquariadise, and the USGS NAS species profile. URL retained for reviewer reference.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder (Shrimp and Snail Breeder) — Cambarellus shufeldtii Detailed Guide
additional.notes: Substitute primary. Source for adult size (~3–3.5 cm), lifespan (~15–18 months, up to 2 years), water parameters (optimum 20–24 °C, broader 18–26 °C; pH 5.4–7.4; gH 3–18; kH 3–15), 20 L minimum tank, omnivorous diet, peaceful but high-escape-risk behavior, easy breeding biology (15–74 eggs/clutch averaging ~45, 3–4 week incubation, ~2 month maturity at 18 mm), and Cambaridae/Decapoda taxonomy.

additional.name: Aquatic Arts — Cajun Dwarf Crayfish product care guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (up to ~4 cm), temperature 16–28 °C, pH 6.0–8.0, KH 6–12, 5-US-gal minimum tank, omnivorous diet, generally peaceful temperament with mild conspecific territoriality, skilled-climber/secure-lid requirement, and overall plant-safe behaviour.

additional.name: Aquariadise — Dwarf Crayfish (Cambarellus) Caresheet
additional.notes: Genus-level cross-check for size (up to ~5 cm across the genus, smaller for shufeldtii), pH 6.5–8, ~30 L (8-US-gal) minimum for small groups, peaceful and community-suitable temperament, and breeding biology (3–4 week egg-carrying period).

additional.name: USGS Nonindigenous Aquatic Species — Cambarellus shufeldtii
additional.notes: Source for native range (Gulf Coastal Plain and lower Mississippi River basin across Louisiana, Arkansas, Mississippi, Alabama, Tennessee, Kentucky, Missouri, southeastern Texas), habitat (swamps, ditches, oxbow ponds, sluggish streams under ~15 inches deep with aquatic vegetation), and non-obligate burrowing behaviour.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Has been reported to molt on the order of once a month as adults, with juveniles molting much more frequently. Soft-bodied post-molt animals tend to hide for several days and the shed exoskeleton is commonly re-consumed for calcium and should not be removed. Like all crustaceans, the species is highly sensitive to copper-based fish medications and snail treatments, which should be avoided entirely.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Generally considered very easy to breed — most pairs reproduce spontaneously in a well-kept tank with adequate cover. See top-level breedingNotes for clutch size, incubation length, and juvenile rearing.
